The Inspection Time task measures your brain's pure perceptual processing speed - how quickly you can perceive and identify visual information.
Tests pure perception, not movement speed
Based on Vickers & Smith (1986) perceptual speed research. Widely used in cognitive aging and clinical neuropsychology.
Two vertical lines will appear very briefly (as short as 50 milliseconds)
A pattern mask appears immediately to prevent afterimage processing
Indicate which line was longer: LEFT or RIGHT
The flash is VERY brief - this is intentional! We're measuring your basic perceptual speed. Don't worry if it seems fast; everyone finds this challenging.
Focus on the center of the screen before each trial
Trust your first impression - don't overthink
The presentation is too brief for detailed analysis
It's okay to guess if you're not sure
Relax - this measures perception, not intelligence
Let's practice with a slower presentation to get familiar with the task.
Great! You understand the task. The actual test will have shorter presentation times.
Excellent perceptual speed! You're processing visual information very quickly and accurately at
ms presentation time. This indicates strong visual processing pathways.
Good perceptual processing! You're accurately perceiving information at brief presentation times. Continued practice will help you process even faster.
Average perceptual speed. Your brain is processing the visual information, but there's room for improvement. Regular practice can enhance your perceptual speed.
Your perceptual processing is developing. This is a challenging task, and improvement comes with practice. We'll adjust the presentation time to match your current abilities.
How quickly your brain can identify and process visual information
Fundamental to many cognitive processes and daily activities
Processing speed is often affected in MS, making this training valuable
Regular practice can enhance your brain's processing efficiency

Inspection Time is a classic cognitive measure that assesses the minimum time needed to perceive and discriminate between simple visual stimuli. It measures pure perceptual processing speed, independent of motor response speed.

The very short presentation time (50-200ms) ensures we're measuring your brain's raw perceptual speed, not your ability to study the image. The immediate mask prevents you from using afterimages or iconic memory, focusing purely on perception.
Perceptual speed slows with age and in neurological conditions
Fundamental to attention, memory, and executive functions
Processing speed deficits are common in MS
Regular practice can improve perceptual efficiency
Tension doesn't help - stay calm and trust your perception
Keep your eyes on the center fixation point
Go with your gut - don't overthink
Your brain will adapt to the brief presentations
Some trials are genuinely at your perceptual threshold
Maintain steady focus throughout the session
Combines accuracy with presentation time to measure processing efficiency. Higher scores indicate faster, more accurate perception.
The task adjusts to find your optimal challenge level, ensuring effective training.
Measures sustained visual attention and dynamic tracking ability, relevant for driving safety and real-world multitasking in MS patients.
Several circles will flash yellow — these are your targets to track
All circles will move randomly. Keep your eyes on the targets!

The yellow circles are your targets to track
Keep your eyes on the targets as they move!

Outstanding processing speed! Your performance of
is well above average. This indicates excellent cognitive efficiency and visual processing ability.
Great performance! Your processing speed of
is above average, showing strong pattern recognition and quick decision-making.
is in the normal range. Regular practice can help improve your pattern recognition speed.
Keep practicing! Processing speed improves significantly with regular training. Pattern comparison tasks are excellent for building quick visual processing and decision-making skills.
Pattern comparison has minimal motor requirements (just clicking), making it an excellent measure of pure cognitive processing speed without physical limitations affecting your score.
Your first impression is often correct. Train yourself to quickly scan both patterns simultaneously rather than examining each in detail.
If unsure, use a systematic method: scan row by row (top to bottom) or column by column (left to right). This ensures you don't miss differences.
The metric "correct per minute" rewards both speed AND accuracy. Going fast but making mistakes won't help your score as much as being quick AND accurate.
With practice, you can detect differences using peripheral vision. Look at the center and let your peripheral vision catch discrepancies.
Your brain gets better at this with practice. Don't overthink - trust your pattern recognition abilities to improve over time.
This task measures pure cognitive processing speed without requiring complex motor skills - perfect for MS assessment. It shows how efficiently your brain processes visual information.

The Useful Field of View (UFOV) test measures your ability to quickly process visual information and divide your attention across your visual field. This is a critical skill for safe driving and everyday activities.
- Predicts driving safety and crash risk
Widely used in MS research to assess visual processing deficits
A fixation cross (+) will appear. Keep your eyes focused on the center.
Images will flash very briefly (150-800ms). Process them quickly!
Report what you saw in the center and/or periphery based on the task.
Identify vehicle in center (car or truck) - Tests processing speed
Identify center vehicle AND locate peripheral shape - Tests divided attention
Same as above but with visual clutter - Tests selective attention
Find this exact item on the screen
Look through all items displayed on screen
Click "Target Present" if you see it, or "Target Absent" if you don't
The target differs by a single attribute (color OR shape). It should "pop out" from the background using parallel visual processing.
The target requires BOTH color AND shape to match. This demands serial visual search and sustained attention.
Is the target present on the screen?
